skill.name: pair-programming
skill.description: AI-assisted pair programming guide for Claude Flow sessions. It documents driver, navigator, switch, TDD, review, mentor, and debug workflows with verification, testing, review, and session management commands.
skill.summary: Run structured AI pair programming sessions with Claude Flow modes, testing, review, and session management.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command examples and several high-severity weak-cryptography patterns. Manual review found no prompt injection, exfiltration, obfuscation, or actual cryptographic implementation; the weak-crypto and reconnaissance hits are keyword false positives. The real risk is that the skill instructs users to install and run Claude Flow commands that may test, commit, push, save sessions, or alter repository state.

medium_findings.title: External Command Workflows Can Modify Project State
medium_findings.description: The skill is mostly documentation, but it repeatedly instructs users to run Claude Flow CLI commands and in-session commands. Some examples include commit, push, auto-commit, configuration import, session save, and background execution, which can modify files, repository history, or session artifacts if followed without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit user-facing workflows, not hidden execution. The risk is real because following them can change repository state or run external tooling, but there is no evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak-cryptography detections correspond to ordinary documentation words and headings such as description, modes, TDD, design, and REST endpoint examples. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or custom cryptographic code in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-level review shows keyword matches inside prose and examples, not cryptographic APIs or algorithm choices. A targeted search found no crypto implementation evidence.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance detections map to benign text such as rapid prototyping, REST API methods, and mode-selection guidance. No evidence found of host enumeration, network scanning, credential discovery, or environment probing instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are normal documentation and REST examples. They do not instruct the model to inspect the host system or collect sensitive information.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Directed External CLI Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides many shell examples for the claude-flow CLI. These commands are visible and user-directed, but marketplace users should review them before running them in a real repository.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external command pattern is confirmed across many documentation examples. It is a moderate operational risk, not hidden command execution.

content.value_statement: Developers need structure when working with AI on complex coding tasks. This skill provides repeatable Claude Flow pair programming workflows for implementation, review, testing, debugging, and learning.
actual_capabilities: Documents driver, navigator, switch, TDD, review, mentor, and debug session modes.
Provides Claude Flow CLI examples for starting, pausing, resuming, ending, saving, and loading sessions.
Defines in-session commands for explanations, suggestions, tests, refactoring, review, commits, and navigation.
Shows configuration examples for default modes, verification thresholds, testing behavior, and session persistence.
Includes practical workflows for authentication, debugging, refactoring, performance work, and API development.
Explains session metrics, troubleshooting steps, keyboard shortcuts, and command history usage.
limitations: Requires Claude Flow CLI and a compatible development environment.
Does not include executable implementation code beyond documented commands and configuration examples.
Command outcomes depend on the target repository, installed tools, and project test setup.
Users must review commit, push, import, auto-commit, and background-session commands before running them.

use_cases.title: Implement a Feature With Guardrails
use_cases.description: Use switch or navigator mode to plan, implement, test, review, and commit a feature in a structured session.

use_cases.title: Debug a Production Issue
use_cases.description: Use debug mode to trace symptoms, inspect likely causes, generate tests, and verify a fix before committing.

use_cases.title: Learn a Codebase Through Pairing
use_cases.description: Use mentor or driver mode to ask for explanations, receive suggestions, and practice changes with review feedback.
use_cases.target_user: Junior developers and new team members

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Session
prompt_templates.prompt: Start a pair programming session for this repository. Use driver mode, explain each step briefly, and ask before running commands that change files.

prompt_templates.title: Build With Tests First
prompt_templates.prompt: Use TDD mode for this feature. Write the failing test plan first, implement the smallest change, run the relevant tests, and summarize verification results.

prompt_templates.title: Review and Refactor Safely
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a review-focused pair programming session on this module. Identify refactoring targets, preserve behavior, add or update tests, and avoid unrelated changes.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a Complex Failure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use debug mode to investigate this failure. Form hypotheses, gather evidence from code and tests, implement the fix, and provide a concise root-cause summary.

output_examples.input: Start a TDD pair programming session for a login rate limit feature.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes a test-first workflow, lists the expected failing tests, guides implementation, runs verification, and summarizes remaining risks.

output_examples.input: Review this service for refactoring opportunities before I commit.
output_examples.output: The assistant reports specific maintainability issues, suggests a scoped refactor plan, asks before modifying files, and verifies behavior with tests.

output_examples.input: Help me debug a memory leak in a Node.js module.
output_examples.output: The assistant narrows possible causes, recommends inspection steps, applies a focused fix, and explains how the change was validated.

best_practices: Start with a clear goal, target files, and preferred session mode.
Review every command that changes files, commits, pushes, imports configuration, or starts background work.
Run relevant tests and summarize verification before ending the session.
anti_patterns: Do not run auto-commit or push workflows without reviewing generated changes first.
Do not use broad refactoring prompts when the task needs a small, auditable change.
Do not rely on truth scores alone as a substitute for tests, review, and project-specific checks.

faq.question: What tools does this skill support?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools. The documented command examples focus on Claude Flow.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code by itself?
faq.answer: No. It is documentation for workflows and commands. Users or agents may run commands only after choosing to follow them.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill contains many external command workflows. Some commands can modify files, commits, configuration, or session data.

faq.question: Were the weak cryptography findings confirmed?
faq.answer: No. Manual review found false positives from ordinary words and headings, not weak cryptographic code.

faq.question: Can I use it for test-driven development?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes TDD mode guidance, coverage examples, test generation commands, and verification workflows.

faq.question: What should I check before using it in a real repository?
faq.answer: Review install commands, generated changes, auto-commit settings, push steps, imported configuration, and background session commands.
